# Implementation and Results

## Introduction

This chapter presents the implementation phase of the system designed in the previous
chapter. We will begin by justifying the choice of a web application, then detail the
programming languages, tools, and frameworks selected, motivating each decision. Finally,
we will illustrate the final result through screenshots of the main interfaces of the
application.


## Justification of the web application choice

The choice to develop a web application, as opposed to a native desktop or mobile
application, rests on several criteria directly related to the context of use of the
system:

- **Universal accessibility**: a web application is accessible from any browser, without
  prior installation, which facilitates adoption by all four categories of actors (students,
  lecturers, administration, companies).
- **Centralised deployment**: a single instance of the system is deployed on a server,
  which simplifies maintenance, updates, and data management.
- **Cross-platform**: the application works independently of the operating system
  (Windows, Linux, macOS) and the type of terminal (computer, tablet, smartphone).
- **Reduced cost**: developing a single codebase covering all terminals avoids the
  duplication of effort inherent in native cross-platform development.
- **Real-time collaboration**: the client-server architecture allows multiple users to
  simultaneously access the system's up-to-date data.

These advantages make the web application the most suitable choice for a PFE management
system intended for a university institution.

## Languages and tools

The implementation of the system relies on a set of languages and tools selected for their
complementarity and suitability to the application's profile.

### Programming languages

- **HTML5** [@mdn2024]: the standard markup language for structuring web page content.
  It constitutes the skeleton of all application views.
- **CSS3** [@mdn2024css]: the stylesheet language used for formatting, layout, and
  responsive design of the user interface.
- **JavaScript (ES2022)** [@ecma2022]: the client-side programming language enabling page
  interactivity, DOM manipulation, and asynchronous communication with the server.
  JavaScript is the only language natively executed by web browsers, making it an
  indispensable choice for the frontend.
- **TypeScript** [@typescript2024]: a typed superset of JavaScript, used server-side
  (Node.js) and client-side to ensure code reliability through static typing and
  compile-time error detection. The choice of TypeScript over plain JavaScript is justified
  by the significant reduction in runtime errors in a project of this scale.
- **Go (Golang)** [@pike2012]: a compiled language designed by Google, used for developing
  the backend REST API. Go was selected for its low memory footprint, native concurrency
  management (goroutines), and the production of a standalone binary deployable without
  external dependencies, which considerably simplifies deployment in a university context.

### Development tools

- **Neovim** [@neovim2024]: the main code editor, highly configurable and extensible,
  used with dedicated plugins for Svelte, TypeScript, and Go.
- **Git / GitHub**: a distributed version control system and code hosting platform,
  enabling change tracking and collaboration.
- **SQLite** [@sqlite2024]: an embedded relational database management system requiring no
  dedicated server. The choice of SQLite over a client-server DBMS (PostgreSQL, MySQL) is
  justified by the simplicity of deployment and the low load expected in a university
  context, while offering ACID compliance and full SQL support.
- **PlantUML**: a tool for generating UML diagrams from textual descriptions, used for
  the modelling presented in the previous chapter.
- **Quarto (LaTeX)**: a scientific publishing system used for writing the present thesis.

## Frameworks

### SvelteKit (Frontend)

SvelteKit [@sveltekit2024] is a full-stack framework based on Svelte [@harris2018], using
Node.js and TypeScript server-side. The choice of SvelteKit over the dominant JavaScript
frameworks (React/Next.js, Vue.js/Nuxt, Angular) is justified by its compilation-based
architecture: code is converted to optimised JavaScript at build time, without a virtual
runtime on the client side. This results in a significantly lower initial load time and
better perceived performance for the user.

Its native handling of server-side rendering (SSR) and file-system-based routing
considerably simplifies development. The application's user interface offers differentiated
views according to the authenticated user's role (student, lecturer, administrator,
company).

### Fiber (Backend)

Fiber [@fiber2024] is a web framework for Go inspired by Express.js, designed for
high-performance applications. It relies on the Fasthttp HTTP engine, which offers
significantly superior performance to Go's standard net/http package. The choice of Fiber
is justified by its ease of use, comprehensive documentation, and middleware ecosystem
(authentication, CORS, logging).

The REST API developed with Fiber exposes secure endpoints and manages business logic as
well as data persistence. The backend architecture follows the **Repository-Service-Handler**
pattern:

- **Repository**: the data access layer (SQL queries to SQLite).
- **Service**: the business logic layer (validation, calculations, orchestration).
- **Handler**: the presentation layer (HTTP endpoints, JSON serialisation).

This strict separation of responsibilities ensures the maintainability, testability, and
scalability of the solution.

## Interface presentation

This section presents screenshots of the main pages of the application, illustrating the
final result of the implementation phase.

### Administration interfaces

The administrator has an overview of the system. As illustrated by @fig-admin-dashboard,
the dashboard allows them to monitor the overall status of PFE processes in a clear and
visual manner.

The first part of this interface presents summary cards displaying the total number of
students (with the number assigned), the number of submitted topics (with those pending or
validated), and available lecturers. This provides an immediate view of key performance
indicators.
Interactive charts then detail the progression of the PFE lifecycle, illustrate student
progression during the academic year as a curve, and break down the overall status of
topics (validated, pending, rejected) through an intuitive pie chart to facilitate
decision-making.

![Administration dashboard, providing an overview of activity, statistics, and PFE progress status.](images/screenshots/admin-dashboard.png){#fig-admin-dashboard width="80%"}

Topic management allows the administration to validate, assign, and filter the various
topics proposed by lecturers and companies. @fig-admin-sujets shows this supervision
interface in detail.

This page is structured around a filterable table ("All", "Pending", "Validated",
"Rejected") listing all PFE proposals. For each topic, the title, required group
composition (individual, pair, trio), the topic initiator with their precise role
(lecturer or company), and the submission date are shown.
In addition, the status column displays colour-coded badges allowing rapid identification
of the topic's state. The action column gives the administrator direct controls: they can,
for example, assign validators to topics awaiting evaluation or unblock rejected topics.

![Topic management interface, enabling the tracking, filtering, and validation of PFE proposals.](images/screenshots/admin-sujets.png){#fig-admin-sujets width="80%"}

Once assignments are complete, the administrator schedules and manages defences. This
interface, presented by @fig-admin-soutenances, centralises upcoming events.

It provides a structured table listing all scheduled or pending defences. The planned date
and time, the assigned room (e.g. "Room A101"), and the current status of the event (e.g.
"Scheduled") are shown. Columns are also provided for the final grade and result once the
defence has taken place.
The administrator has filtering tools at the top of the page by date and by status, as
well as a "Schedule a Defence" creation button. Each table row includes a "View" button
giving access to the specific details of the appointment, jury, and assessed group.

![Defence planning and management interface.](images/screenshots/admin-soutenances.png){#fig-admin-soutenances width="80%"}

### Lecturer interfaces

The lecturer dashboard provides quick access to their supervisions, the topics they propose,
and the juries to which they are assigned. @fig-teacher-dashboard illustrates this
personalised home page.

The top of the interface highlights four key indicators: the number of students they
supervise, the topics awaiting validation, the topics they have already proposed, and their
availability status ("Available" by default). Quick access links ("Shortcuts") in the form
of clearly highlighted buttons allow them to navigate directly to "My Topics", "Topics to
Validate", or to "Edit Availability".
In the centre of the screen, two panels inform the lecturer of crucial events: "Upcoming
Defences", which lists their convocations as a jury member (with date, time, and location),
and "Notifications", for unread and recent messages or alerts.

![Lecturer dashboard summarising responsibilities, supervisions, juries, and proposals at a glance.](images/screenshots/teacher-dashboard.png){#fig-teacher-dashboard width="80%"}

The topics page allows the lecturer to submit new topics via a dedicated form and to
consult the validation status of their proposals, as shown by @fig-teacher-sujets.

This view lists in table form the complete history of topics created by the lecturer. Each
row shows the title of the proposal (e.g. "AI Chatbot for student services"), the required
group size (Pair), the current status indicated by a visual badge (e.g. "PENDING"), and
the proposal date.
A main action button "Propose a new topic" is prominently displayed at the top right to
enable new submissions easily. This centralises and simplifies the PFE submission process
for teaching teams.

![Interface for managing topics proposed by the lecturer.](images/screenshots/teacher-sujets.png){#fig-teacher-sujets width="80%"}

### Student interfaces

Upon login, the student accesses a summary dashboard indicating important dates and the
status of their PFE clearly and concisely. @fig-student-dashboard gives an overview of
this interface.

The layout focuses on the essentials: statistical indicators show the status of their PFE
(currently "IN PROGRESS"), the number of preferences they have already submitted for their
topic choices, and their unread notifications. All these elements are accessible at a
single glance.
The student also has dedicated sections for their latest notifications and quick shortcuts
to consult the global topic catalogue, manage their preferences, or access the detailed
monitoring space for their PFE. These shortcuts facilitate smooth navigation without
getting lost in secondary menus.

![Student dashboard, displaying the search status, submitted preferences, and shortcuts to the main actions.](images/screenshots/student-dashboard.png){#fig-student-dashboard width="80%"}

To express their choices, the student has access to the full catalogue and can submit
their preferences. @fig-student-voeux shows the preference management interface.

On this screen, the student can view and manage the ranked list of topics they are
interested in. The system clearly indicates the allowed limit (e.g. "1 preference out of
5 maximum"), providing a visual constraint that helps limit errors.
Submitted preferences are displayed as information cards detailing the topic title, its
identifier, its status, and its creation/addition date. For each choice, the student has
the option to "Remove" it with a single click if they change their mind before the
assignment period closes.

![Preference expression and management page for the student.](images/screenshots/student-voeux.png){#fig-student-voeux width="80%"}

The student's PFE space allows them to monitor the progress of their project. They can
consult their supervisor's comments and interact chronologically, as illustrated by
@fig-student-pfe.

This view centralises information around the validated project: the "Topic" status with
the assigned supervisor (or unassigned) and a possible co-supervisor (e.g. a "Company")
are shown.
Below, the "Progress Log" lists exchanges and the history of meetings. The student can
add new interactions by entering the "Meeting Date" and writing descriptive "Notes" on the
progress of their work. This digital logbook is essential for tracing task evolution and
stakeholder involvement.

![The "My PFE" space allowing the student to monitor their project progress and maintain a logbook.](images/screenshots/student-pfe.png){#fig-student-pfe width="80%"}

### Company interfaces

Industrial partners access the platform via a dedicated registration process: the company
creates an account by entering their details (name, sector of activity, contact person),
and this account is then subject to administrative verification before activation. Once
validated by the administration, the company can log in and access their personalised
space. Partners benefit from a tailored interface adapted to their needs, notably using an
aesthetic and restful dark mode. @fig-company-dashboard shows this dashboard.

For other profiles, the view presents company activity indicators: "Proposed Topics",
"Supervised Students", and "Unread Notifications". This provides an immediate summary of
their involvement in the institution's PFE programme.
Specific panels display an overview of ongoing supervisions (currently empty if there are
none yet) and the status of the latest topics submitted for validation. A "Propose a
topic" shortcut is easily accessible, encouraging and streamlining the submission of
industrial problem statements.

![Company partner dashboard in dark mode, summarising proposals and supervisions.](images/screenshots/company-dashboard.png){#fig-company-dashboard width="80%"}

Topic management allows the company to consult the history of their proposals and verify
their validation status by the scientific committee. @fig-company-sujets shows this topic
list.

This table summarises all the company's submissions. Relevant details are shown, such as
the title ("Cloud migration of infrastructure"), the domains, the required group type, and
its status (e.g. "PENDING").
This view emphasises the readability of statuses to avoid the company having to constantly
contact the administration: they can see directly whether their proposal has been accepted,
is under review, or has been validated.

![Interface for managing topics submitted by the partner company.](images/screenshots/company-sujets.png){#fig-company-sujets width="80%"}

The company can also formulate and submit new project proposals via a structured form
facilitating evaluation by the institution. This form is visible in @fig-company-proposal.

The form groups all mandatory fields marked with an asterisk: a text field for the "Topic
Title", a detailed "Description" area for listing objectives and expected technologies, as
well as dropdown menus to specify the "Level", "Specialisation", and "Group Type" (e.g.
Individual - 1 student).
An informative note subtly reminds users that the topic will only be visible in the
catalogue after validation by specialist lecturers, reinforcing the transparency of the
process. Once the data is entered, the main action button allows the proposal to be
finalised.

![New PFE topic proposal form for the company, including all required descriptive fields.](images/screenshots/company-proposal.png){#fig-company-proposal width="100%"}

## Conclusion

This chapter presented the concrete implementation of the system designed in the previous
chapter. The choice of a web application is justified by universal accessibility,
centralised deployment, and cross-platform compatibility. The client-server architecture
combining SvelteKit (frontend) and Go/Fiber (backend) made it possible to develop a
performant, maintainable application adapted to the Algerian university context. The
developed interfaces cover all the identified functionalities: authentication, topic
management, expression of preferences, assignment, pedagogical supervision, defence
scheduling, and grading.
